brief.status: The project is simultaneously in Site Plan Review before the Planning Board and under a Notice of Intent (DEP File No. 14-1452) before the Conservation Commission; the most recent event is a Planning Board hearing on June 9, 2026, with no continuance date stated.

events.summary: The Commission continued the Request for Determination of Applicability for a 10.4-acre R&D campus at 54 Cherry Hill Drive to April 9, 2026, pending a site visit. At least one commissioner indicated a preference for a full Order of Conditions rather than a negative determination given the scale of buffer disturbance.

stories.name: Humphrey Street Overlay District Ground-Floor Commercial Preservation Amendment

brief.what: A proposed zoning amendment to the Humphrey Street Overlay District in Swampscott, MA that would require discretionary review — and potentially a special permit — before ground-floor commercial space in mixed-use buildings can be converted to residential use.
brief.status: The amendment is at a preliminary discussion stage; the Planning Board reviewed the concept at its June 8, 2026 meeting with no formal public hearing yet scheduled.
brief.whats_next: No formal hearing date is stated in the record; the Board indicated intent to develop draft language in coordination with broader master planning efforts for a future Town Meeting.
brief.outlook: Momentum appears cautious but directional — the Board expressed support for drafting language as early as May and returned to the topic within a month, suggesting the concept has internal traction. Progress will likely depend on how quickly draft language is produced and whether it is tied to an upcoming Town Meeting warrant cycle.

events.summary: Planning Board discussed a potential zoning amendment to the Humphrey Street Overlay District to prevent ground-floor commercial space in mixed-use buildings from being converted to residential use without discretionary review. The discussion was prompted by recent inquiries and proposals to convert corridor properties to multifamily residential. Strategies discussed include requiring ground-floor commercial use in key segments and mandating a special permit for commercial-to-residential conversions; Board expressed support for developing draft language for a future Town Meeting in coordination with broader master planning efforts.

The Planning Board will discuss a potential zoning amendment to protect existing ground-floor commercial space in mixed-use buildings. This is a preliminary discussion with no formal hearing scheduled.

stories.name: 74 Beaver Street Lot Split and Two-Family Townhouse

brief.what: Eric Duhaime's property at 74 Beaver Street in Salem, MA is being subdivided into two nonconforming lots — a 4,502-sq-ft parcel fronting Beaver Street and a 5,621-sq-ft parcel fronting Silver Street — to allow construction of a new three-bedroom two-family townhouse on the rear lot.
brief.status: All land-use approvals are in place: the ZBA granted setback and frontage variances in March 2026, and the Planning Board approved both a frontage waiver and an ANR endorsement on June 4, 2026, completing the lot-split process.
brief.whats_next: A building permit cannot be issued until the applicant submits a revised plot plan demonstrating three code-compliant parking spaces, as conditioned by the ZBA.
brief.outlook: With ZBA and Planning Board approvals secured and only the parking-plan condition standing between the project and a building permit, the project appears to be on a straightforward path to construction — assuming the plot plan is revised without complication.

events.summary: ZBA approved a variance to subdivide 74 Beaver Street into two nonconforming lots — a 4,502-sq-ft parcel along Beaver Street and a 5,621-sq-ft parcel along Silver Street — and to construct a new three-bedroom two-family townhouse with a 3-ft front setback and 12-ft rear setback. A condition requires a revised plot plan showing three code-compliant parking spaces before a building permit is issued.

Planning Board approved a waiver of frontage requirements under the Subdivision Control Law at 74 Beaver Street, permitting 58.64 feet of frontage where 100 feet is required, to enable a lot split for construction of a two-family home at the rear of the parcel. The ZBA had separately approved the two-family dwelling with reduced frontage and setback variances; access to the rear lot is from Silver Street.

Planning Board endorsed an Approval Not Required (ANR) plan under the Subdivision Control Law for 74 Beaver Street, completing the lot-split process that will allow construction of a two-family home at the rear of the parcel pursuant to prior ZBA approval.

stories.name: 50 Washington Street — Tabernacle Congregational Church Renovation

brief.what: Beacon Communities Services LLC proposes to renovate the existing Tabernacle Congregational Church at 50 Washington Street in Salem's B5 zoning district and construct two additions within existing parking lots to create 48 studio apartments for veterans, while retaining existing church uses in the building.
brief.status: The Site Plan Review and Planned Unit Development Special Permit hearing has been continued four times and currently awaits a Planning Board session scheduled for June 25, 2026.
brief.whats_next: The public hearing is continued to the June 25, 2026 Planning Board meeting.
brief.outlook: The project has cleared procedural votes unanimously (7-0) at each continuation, suggesting board support, but unresolved peer-review items — a required pervious strip and curb-cut configuration across three street frontages — have driven repeated deferrals; resolution of those technical comments will likely determine whether a decision is reached at the next hearing.

events.summary: Beacon Communities Services LLC seeks Site Plan Review and a Planned Unit Development Special Permit to renovate the existing Tabernacle Congregational Church building and construct two additions within existing parking lots to create 48 studio apartments for veterans. Nine car-parking spaces and utility and landscaping improvements are also proposed; existing church uses will be retained. Continued to the April 23, 2026 meeting on a 7-0 vote.

Beacon Communities Services LLC proposes to renovate the Tabernacle Congregational Church building and add two new additions within existing parking lots to create 48 studio apartments for veterans at 50 Washington Street in the B5 (Central Development) district, while retaining existing church uses in the building. The Site Plan Review and Planned Unit Development Special Permit hearing was continued to May 21, 2026.

Continuation of public hearing for Beacon Communities' proposal to renovate the existing Tabernacle Congregational Church and add two new building additions within current parking lots to create 48 studio apartments for veterans, while maintaining existing church uses; the project includes 9 parking spaces and site improvements. Discussion focused on two dimensional peer-review findings: a required two-foot pervious strip between parking line and pervious surface, and curb-cut totals across frontage on Washington, Federal, and Lynde Streets (the Lynde Street cut to be removed). Board voted 7-0 to continue to June 4, 2026 pending peer-review response.

Planning Board continued a public hearing on Beacon Communities' proposal to renovate the Tabernacle Congregational Church and construct two additions within existing parking lots to create 48 studio apartments for veterans in the B5 (Central Development) zoning district. The project includes 9 car parking spaces plus utility and landscaping improvements; existing church uses will be retained within the building. Continued to June 25, 2026.

stories.name: Pole Petition No. 20260113-1 – Maple Street Utility Pole Replacement
stories.address: Maple Street near House #395, Danvers, MA 01923

events.summary: Public hearing on joint Danvers Electric and Verizon petition (No. 20260113-1, dated January 13, 2026) to replace one utility pole along with wires, cable, fixtures, anchors, and guys on Maple Street near house #395 (pole 4-87-1). Danvers Electric is the town's municipal electric utility, making this a public-agency-initiated infrastructure action.

brief.what: Residential construction at 86 Elliott Street in Danvers, MA involving a 900 sq ft two-bedroom ADU above a garage, filed under Notice of Intent DEP File No. 14-1450 with the Conservation Commission by Majlinda and Spartak Vaka.
brief.status: The hearing remained open as of May 28, 2026, with a Conservation Commission Enforcement Order also on that agenda; the structure encroaches into both the 35-ft no-disturb and 50-ft no-build buffer zones of an intermittent stream, and the applicant had agreed at the prior hearing to revise plans to shift the building and driveway further from the stream.
brief.whats_next: No next step stated in the record.
brief.outlook: The project faces significant regulatory headwinds: work began without the required Conservation permit, the as-built footprint breaches two protective buffer zones, and an enforcement order was pending as of the last record. The trajectory suggests the applicant will need to demonstrate meaningful plan revisions before the Commission is likely to issue an Order of Conditions, and formal enforcement action remains a live possibility.

events.summary: Conservation Commission continued the Notice of Intent for new residential construction at 86 Elliott Street (DEP #14-1450) to April 23, 2026, pending a site visit. The homeowners began clearing and foundation work under a building permit before discovering the site's proximity to an intermittent stream; work was stopped, and the proposal now includes a permeable driveway and stormwater infiltration system.
Notice of Intent (DEP File No. 14-1450) for 86 Elliott Street was continued to May 14, 2026 at the applicant's request. No project scope or nature of work was described in these minutes.

Conservation Commission continued the Notice of Intent hearing for an unpermitted 900 sq ft ADU (two-bedroom unit above garage) at 86 Elliott Street after finding that the structure encroaches into both the 35-ft no-disturb and 50-ft no-build buffer zones of an intermittent stream, with 2,287 sq ft impacted in the no-disturb zone and 3,296 sq ft in the no-build zone. The applicant began construction after receiving a building permit without knowing a Conservation permit was required and agreed to revise plans to shift the building and driveway further from the stream before the May 28 hearing.
Notice of Intent filed with the Conservation Commission for work at 86 Elliott Street (DEP File No. 14-1450). Project scope is not described in the agenda.
Conservation Commission Enforcement Order on the agenda for 86 Elliott Street, owned by Majlinda Vaka. No detail on the nature of the violation is provided in the agenda.
Notice of Intent filed with the Conservation Commission for work at 32 Centre Street (DEP File No. 14-1453). Project scope is not described in the agenda.
Notice of Intent filed with the Conservation Commission for work at 267 Centre Street (DEP File No. 14-1454). Project scope is not described in the agenda.
Notice of Intent filed with the Conservation Commission for work at 28 Brentwood Circle (DEP File No. 14-145, final digit truncated in source). Project scope is not described in the agenda.

stories.name: National Grid Conduit Installation - Lafayette Street and College Drive
stories.address: Lafayette St and College Drive, Salem, MA (service point: 352 Lafayette St)

brief.what: National Grid petitioned Salem's City Council for permission to install underground conduits on Lafayette Street at College Drive to provide electrical service to 352 Lafayette Street.
brief.status: The City Council approved National Grid's petition on May 28, 2026 following a public hearing that drew no opposition; the location was granted by vote.
brief.whats_next: No next step stated in the record.
brief.outlook: The petition moved from hearing to unanimous approval in two weeks with no recorded opposition, suggesting the installation phase faces no apparent regulatory obstacles.

events.summary: National Grid petitioned the City Council for permission to install conduits on Lafayette Street and College Drive to provide electrical service to 352 Lafayette Street. A public hearing was ordered for May 28, 2026.

City Council approved National Grid's petition to install underground conduits on Lafayette Street at College Drive to provide electrical service to 352 Lafayette Street. A public hearing was held with no opposition; the location was granted by vote.

stories.name: School and Municipal Building Stabilization Fund — Establishment

events.summary: City Council unanimously established a School and Municipal Building Stabilization Fund under MGL Chapter 40, Section 5B to accumulate revenues for future planning, design, acquisition, construction, renovation, repair, and maintenance of Salem's municipal and school buildings, including payment of debt service on bonds or notes issued for such purposes. Balances do not revert to the general fund at year-end.

stories.name: Home Rule Petition — Increase Short-Term Rental Community Impact Fee to 6%

events.summary: City Council approved 10-0 a home rule petition to the General Court authorizing Salem to raise the community impact fee on professionally managed and owner-adjacent short-term rental units from 3% to up to 6% of total rent per occupancy. Revenue from the additional 3-percentage-point increment would be deposited in a special fund restricted to school and municipal building capital needs.

stories.name: Home Rule Petition — Increase Local Lodging Excise Tax to 8.75%

events.summary: City Council approved 10-0 a home rule petition authorizing Salem to increase the local lodging excise on hotels, motels, bed-and-breakfasts, and short-term rentals from the current 6% cap to up to 8.75%. Revenue from the additional 2.75-percentage-point increment would be held in a separate special fund dedicated to school and municipal facility capital projects.

stories.name: South Essex Sewerage District Centennial Plan — Wastewater Infrastructure Legislation
stories.address: South Essex Sewerage District, Salem, MA

events.summary: City Council referred to Committee of the Whole a resolution supporting SESD's filing of state legislation to address aging wastewater infrastructure and rising operating costs. The proposed legislation would authorize the Centennial Plan with a $390 million cost cap (Tiers 1 and 2), allow bonding and borrowing to finance the work, establish a non-exempt FY2028 budget ceiling of $30.5 million with 2.5% annual escalation, and exempt Centennial Plan construction from Proposition 2½ limits; without relief SESD warns of permit violations and potential EPA consent order affecting Salem Sound.

Applicant seeks a variance under §300-37 D(8) to demolish and reconstruct a pre-existing nonconforming accessory structure as an accessory dwelling unit, with a rear yard setback encroachment into the RHD district. Creates one new dwelling unit on an existing residential lot.

B. Dupont-Raesly, LLC is before the Planning Board for Special Permits and a Waiver to expand an existing building at 103 High Street in the High Street Mixed Use Corridor district. The application addresses urban-form requirements including Build-To-Zone, side yard setback, street-facing transparency for both High Street and Healy Court frontages, and outdoor amenity space.

brief.what: Maple Circle Marblehead LLC seeks a Special Permit or Variance from the Zoning Board of Appeals to subdivide 9 Maple Circle in Marblehead's Single Residence District into two lots: Lot 1 retains the existing dwelling with substandard front and side yard setbacks, and Lot 2 would receive a new single-family dwelling with substandard frontage and lot width.
brief.status: The application has been continued twice — from January 27 to March 24, then to May 26, 2026 — with no evidence taken at either hearing.
brief.whats_next: No next step or hearing date is stated in the record.
brief.outlook: Three continuances with no evidence taken suggests the applicant or board is not yet ready to proceed on the merits; momentum appears slow, and further continuances are plausible before substantive review begins.

events.summary: Maple Circle Marblehead LLC seeks a special permit or variance to divide 9 Maple Circle into two building lots in the Single Residence District: Lot 1 retains the existing dwelling (substandard front and side yard setbacks) and Lot 2 would receive a new single-family dwelling (substandard frontage and lot width). The matter was continued from January 27, 2026 with no evidence taken.

Maple Circle Marblehead LLC seeks a Special Permit or Variance to divide 9 Maple Circle into two building lots, each with dimensional non-conformities, and to construct a new single-family dwelling on the newly created Lot 2. The application was continued from March 24, 2026 with no evidence yet taken.

Nathaniel Walton seeks a Special Permit to demolish an existing non-conforming single-family home at 340 Ocean Avenue and replace it with a new 2.5-story single-family residence on the same non-conforming lot in the Expanded Single Residence District. The rebuilt home will not meet lot area requirements and will encroach into the side and rear yard setbacks.

Stephanie Brocoum seeks Special Permit(s) on court-ordered remand to construct a rear addition to an existing non-conforming two-story commercial building at 132 Washington Street and convert the second floor from office to residential use, creating a mixed-use (commercial and residential) property in the Unrestricted District. The rear addition will encroach on the side yard setback.

stories.name: Proposed Amendments to Beverly Zoning Ordinance — Accessory Dwelling Units

events.summary: The Beverly Planning Board will hold a public hearing on proposed amendments to the City Zoning Ordinance regarding accessory dwelling units (ADUs). No draft text, specific provisions, or scope of the amendment are described in the agenda.

Danvers issued a public bid for furnishing all labor and equipment for the application of reflectorized painted pavement markings. Bidders are required to be prequalified by MassDOT in the 'Pavement Markings' category. Bid documents and submission are handled through ProjectDog project code 874485; the bid closed on 5/21/2026.

stories.name: Forest River Residences Phase 1 (AvalonBay Communities)
stories.address: 8 Harrison Road, 9 Harrison Road, 262 Loring Avenue, 266 Loring Avenue, Salem, MA

brief.what: Phase 1 of the Forest River Residences, proposed by AvalonBay Communities, Inc., consists of a 340-unit residential building with an above-grade parking garage (524 spaces), two enclosed outdoor courtyards, an approximately half-acre landscaped common, an extension of Harrison Road, and a connection to the Forest River Conservation Trailhead, on former Salem State University South Campus parcels at 8 Harrison Road, 9 Harrison Road, 262 Loring Avenue, and 266 Loring Avenue in Salem, MA.
brief.status: The Planning Board approved the Smart Growth Overlay District plan review on April 23, 2026, by a 7-0 vote with 2 abstentions.
brief.whats_next: No next step stated in the record.
brief.outlook: Phase 1 secured unanimous Planning Board approval after two continuances and peer review, suggesting the project cleared its primary land-use hurdle without significant opposition; the trajectory points toward advancement to permitting, though the record does not indicate a construction timeline.

contacts.name: Joseph C. Correnti, Esq. f/b/o WinnDevelopment Companies, LLC

events.summary: AvalonBay Communities proposes Phase 1 of the two-phase Forest River Residences: a new residential building with 340 units, an above-grade parking garage with 524 spaces, two enclosed outdoor courtyards, a half-acre landscaped common, and an extension of Harrison Road with a connection to the Forest River Conservation Trailhead. The project has Design Review Board approval; peer review is expected to conclude before the April 9, 2026 hearing where a vote is anticipated, with the 120-day clock expiring May 7.

WinnDevelopment proposes Phase 2 of the Forest River Residences: adaptive reuse of three former Salem State University South Campus buildings (Harrington, Academic, and Alumni) plus one new four-story residential building along Harrison Road, for a total of up to 145 dwelling units, 159 surface parking spaces, and landscaped pedestrian common areas. A joint traffic study covering both phases was presented, projecting approximately 160–165 peak-hour vehicle trips; the matter was continued to April 9, 2026, with the 120-day clock expiring June 4.

Continued joint public hearing before the Planning Board and Design Review Board for Smart Growth Overlay District Plan Review and C-ROD Site Plan Review. The applicant proposes up to 145 dwelling units through adaptive reuse of three existing Salem State University South Campus buildings and construction of one new four-story residential building along Harrison Road, with 159 surface parking spaces and landscaped common areas. This is Phase 2 of the Forest River Residences project.

Continued public hearing for Smart Growth Overlay District Plan Review and C-ROD Site Plan Review for Phase 1 of the Forest River Residences. The applicant proposes a 340-unit residential building with an above-grade parking garage (524 spaces), two enclosed outdoor courtyards, an approximately half-acre landscaped common, an extension of Harrison Road, and a connection to the Forest River Conservation Trailhead.

AvalonBay Communities received Smart Growth Overlay District plan approval (7-0, 2 abstentions) for Phase 1 of the Forest River Residences: a 340-unit residential building with an above-level parking garage (524 spaces), two outdoor courtyards, and an approximately half-acre landscaped common on former Salem State University South Campus parcels, including extension of Harrison Road and a connection to the Forest River Conservation Trailhead with five dedicated parking spaces.

WinnDevelopment Companies proposes up to 145 dwelling units through adaptive reuse of three former Salem State University South Campus buildings (Harrington, Academic, and Alumni) plus a new four-story residential building along Harrison Road, with 159 surface parking spaces and landscaped pedestrian paths — the second phase of the Forest River Residences. The Smart Growth Overlay District plan review hearing was continued to May 7, 2026.

Continuation of public hearing for WinnDevelopment's up-to-145-unit affordable residential project proposing adaptive reuse of three former Salem State University South Campus buildings (Harrington, Academic, and Alumni) plus one new four-story building along Harrison Road, with 159 surface parking spaces and landscaped common areas. Key open issues include resolution of stormwater peer-review comments, MassDOT access-permit stormwater requirements (including a contested detention basin that would require tree removal on Route 1A), and outstanding architectural and traffic submittals. Board voted 7-0 to continue to June 25, 2026.

stories.name: House of the Seven Gables Floodproofing and Site Improvements

brief.what: The House of the Seven Gables Settlement Association is undertaking Phase 1A of a climate adaptation plan at 115 Derby St, Salem, MA, involving relocation of a historic counting house and a boat play structure to more inland positions on the property, installation of floodproofing elements, and restoration and realignment of visitor pathways and landscaping.
brief.status: The Zoning Board of Appeals approved a parking finding for the project on 2026-05-20, accepting that the relocation of the counting house — which displaces nine parking spaces — still leaves the reconfigured lot compliant with ordinance requirements; a Conservation Commission public hearing on the Notice of Intent was opened the prior day and remains open in the record.
brief.whats_next: No next step stated in the record.
brief.outlook: With the ZBA parking hurdle cleared and the Conservation Commission hearing already opened, both major regulatory tracks appear to be advancing in parallel; the project's momentum looks favorable, though Conservation Commission approval has not yet been recorded.

contacts.name: House of the Seven Gables Settlement Association

events.summary: Public hearing on a Notice of Intent for relocation of the Counting House and a boat play structure to inland locations, installation of floodproofing elements, and restoration and realignment of visitor pathways and landscaping at 115 Derby St within a wetlands resource area.

The ZBA approved a parking finding for the House of Seven Gables Settlement Association's Phase 1A climate adaptation plan, which relocates a small historic counting house from a vulnerable harbor-edge position to a more protected area on the property. The relocation displaces nine parking spaces; the Board found the reconfigured lot still satisfies ordinance requirements for employee and visitor parking at this museum-classified property.

The ZBA approved parking variances for 2 Bridge, LLC to operate a 99-seat restaurant (52 inside, 42 outside seats plus 5 staff) on the first floor of an existing waterfront building in the B2/Entrance Corridor Overlay District. The variances allow 12–18 on-site parking spaces where 28 are required, including undersized spaces abutting the property line; a special condition requires the petitioner to work with the Transportation Department to identify and sign compact-car spaces per the April 24, 2026 Griffin Engineering Group parking plan.

The ZBA approved a finding for 33 School Street LLC to convert the sloped-roof attic of a nonconforming two-family structure into a 647-square-foot accessory dwelling unit, creating a third unit in the R2 district. The project involves no change to the building's footprint or exterior massing.

The ZBA approved a variance and ADU finding for Jacob Levine to construct a second-story addition above an existing CMU garage, resulting in a 2.5-story, 24-foot-tall accessory dwelling unit sited at zero feet from the nonconforming lot's side and rear property lines in the R1/Coastal Resiliency Overlay District. Special conditions require removal of windows facing the abutting property and addition of a minimum six-foot privacy screen along the eastern setback.

The Select Board will receive a Downtown update presented jointly by DPW Director Stephen King and Land Use & Community Services Director Aaron Henry. No project scope, cost, or specific improvements are described in the agenda.

stories.name: Glen Urquhart School Building Raze and Rebuild

brief.what: Glen Urquhart School proposes to raze an existing building at 206 Greenwood Avenue in Beverly, MA and replace it with a structure housing two classrooms, an exhibition hall, and administrative and meeting space, with a net reduction of over 1,000 sq ft of impervious surface and invasive species removal with native replanting.
brief.status: The Beverly Conservation Commission closed its public hearing on May 19, 2026 and issued an Order of Conditions approving the project, subject to invasive species management with two years of annual monitoring, dewatering controls during construction, and 75% planting survivorship as a condition of the final Certificate of Compliance.
brief.whats_next: The project must satisfy construction-phase conditions — dewatering with a sediment bag, invasive species management, and achieving 75% planting survivorship — before a Certificate of Compliance will be issued.
brief.outlook: With Conservation Commission approval secured and conditions clearly defined, the project appears positioned to move into permitting and construction without significant regulatory obstacles; the two-year post-construction monitoring requirement means the compliance timeline will extend well past project completion.

contacts.name: Glen Urquhart School c/o Lindsay Calef

events.summary: Beverly Conservation Commission opened and continued a Notice of Intent hearing for Glen Urquhart School's proposal to raze an existing building and construct a replacement at 206 Greenwood Avenue. The new building would house two classrooms, an exhibition hall, and administrative/meeting space, with a net reduction of over 1,000 sq ft of impervious surface across all buffer zones and the riverfront area; invasive species removal and native replanting are included as mitigation. A Commission site walk is scheduled for May 2, 2026, and the hearing is continued to May 19, 2026.

The Conservation Commission closed the public hearing and issued an Order of Conditions permitting Glen Urquhart School to raze and rebuild an existing building at 206 Greenwood Avenue. Conditions include invasive species management with two years of annual monitoring, dewatering with a sediment bag during construction, and 75% planting survivorship before a Certificate of Compliance will be issued.

stories.name: Brooks Street Drainage Rehabilitation
stories.address: Brooks Street between Jefferson Ave and Lawrence St, Salem, MA (Map 24)

events.summary: Public hearing on a Request for Determination of Applicability for rehabilitation of existing drainage on Brooks Street, including replacement of existing drainage features and installation of new features within a wetlands resource area.

The Commission issued a Negative 3 Determination clearing the proposal to create an accessory dwelling unit within the existing house at 23 Virginia Avenue. The project involves constructing an exterior enclosed staircase addition on helical piers at the rear of the house providing secondary egress to the second-floor ADU, sited more than 25 feet from the bordering vegetated wetland.

brief.what: Forest River Realty, LLC is seeking to convert an existing two-family residence at 13 Popes Lane in Danvers, MA into a contractor's yard, involving demolition of the older portion of the house, retention of the addition as an office, 15 crushed-stone parking spaces, a bulk storage area, a dumpster pad, and a small retaining wall in the Highway Corridor Zone.
brief.status: The Conservation Commission issued an order of conditions approving the plan on May 14, 2026, with a three-year limit; the Planning Board Special Permit and Site Plan Review hearing was held May 12, 2026, but no decision from that board appears in the record.
brief.whats_next: Two parking spaces must be relocated out of the no-disturb zone and an updated plan submitted to the Conservation Commission before the order is formally filed.
brief.outlook: Conservation Commission approval with conditions suggests the project has cleared a significant regulatory hurdle, and the minor relocation requirement is a defined, bounded task rather than a substantive redesign — both indicators of forward momentum. Final status depends on the Planning Board's Special Permit decision, which the record does not yet show.

events.summary: Forest River Realty, LLC is seeking a Special Permit under Section 30.5 and Site Plan Review under Section 4 of the Zoning Bylaw to construct a contractor's yard with associated site modifications at 13 Popes Lane in the Highway Corridor Zone (HCZ). No square footage or dollar value was stated in the agenda.

Conservation Commission issued an order of conditions approving Forest River Realty's plan to convert an existing two-family residence at 13 Popes Lane into a contractor yard, involving demolition of the older portion of the house, retention of the addition as an office, installation of 15 parking spaces on crushed stone, a bulk storage area, a dumpster pad, and a small retaining wall, with approximately 167 sq ft of buffer-zone impact. Two parking spaces must be relocated out of the no-disturb zone and an updated plan submitted before the order is formally filed; the order carries a three-year limit.

Request for Certificate of Compliance for 80 Andover Street (DEP File No. 14-0516), submitted by Seaside Legal Solutions on behalf of owner John Kiley, Jr. A Certificate of Compliance closes out a prior Order of Conditions; no information on the original permitted work scope is provided in the agenda.

Town of Danvers is procuring crack sealing and micro surfacing services under Bid 2026-25. Bid documents are available through ProjectDog at project code 874330; submissions must be made via ProjectDog at project code 873970. MassDOT prequalification in crack sealing is required.

The Town of Danvers solicited bids for rehabilitation of approximately 8,100 linear feet of sanitary sewers, including excavation spot repairs, rehabilitation of approximately 55 manholes, lateral connection lining of approximately 20 sewer services, and sewer service grouting. Bid documents were available through ProjectDog at project code 873970; the bid closed May 14, 2026.

stories.name: FY27 Water and Sewer Capital Improvement Program - Bond Authorization

brief.what: A $7,660,000 bond authorization for the FY27 water and sewer capital improvement program in Salem, MA, covering sewer pipe relocation on Willow Avenue, Canal Street water main improvements, Bridge Street drainage, Jefferson and Ocean Avenue utility repairs, water transmission main system improvements, and citywide sewer infiltration/inflow elimination and cleaning.
brief.status: On May 14, 2026, the City Council referred the bond authorization to the Committee on Admin & Finance; no committee action is yet on record.
brief.whats_next: Review and a recommendation from the Committee on Admin & Finance before any full council vote.
brief.outlook: Referral to committee is a standard first step and does not indicate opposition; the specificity and breadth of the listed projects suggest this is a well-scoped program, which tends to support smoother committee review, though no timeline or vote date is stated in the record.

events.summary: The City Council referred a $7,660,000 bond authorization for FY27 water and sewer capital improvements to the Committee on Admin & Finance. Projects include Willow Avenue sewer pipe relocation ($1M), Canal Street water main improvements ($1M), Bridge Street drainage ($800K), Jefferson Avenue and Ocean Avenue utility repairs ($1.3M), water transmission main system improvements ($1.2M), citywide sewer infiltration/inflow elimination ($610K), and citywide drain/sewer cleaning and inspection ($525K).

The City Council referred a $7,862,000 bond authorization for FY27 general fund capital improvements. Largest items include Transfer Station capping ($1.6M), citywide roads/sidewalks/crosswalks ($900K), school technology ($810K), school MEP and HVAC ($600K), DPS Sidewalk Implementation Project ($600K), school security and life safety infrastructure ($700K), Salem Street sidewalk and crosswalk improvements ($500K), Winter Island building stabilization ($500K), Forest River Park main bathroom ($400K), school playgrounds ($400K), High Street Park renovation ($325K), and Jefferson/Jackson intersection design ($227K).

stories.name: FY27 Water and Sewer CIP - Retained Earnings Projects

events.summary: The City Council referred a $1,080,000 appropriation from Water/Sewer Fund Retained Earnings for utility repairs at specific locations: Chestnut Street sewer and utility repairs from Summer Street to Cambridge Street ($650K), Winthrop Street sewer work ($130K), sewer infrastructure repair ($100K), and water infrastructure repair and replacement ($200K).

The FY27 budget funds design studies for four named road corridors totaling $680,000: Jefferson/Jackson intersection design ($227K from bond authorization), Willson Street design ($220K), Proctor Street Corridor from Mansell to Highland ($154K), and Proctor/Pope/Mansell intersection design ($79K). These design phases precede future street reconstruction and are funded across the bond authorization and Capital Outlay Fund.
The FY27 Capital Outlay Fund includes $250,000 for a Master Plan Update under the Planning department. No scope detail or timeline beyond the appropriation is provided in the agenda.

stories.name: Horace Mann Laboratory School Decarbonization and Electrification Feasibility Study

events.summary: City Council adopted a $250,000 appropriation for feasibility study and schematic design of an accelerated repair project at Horace Mann Laboratory School, 79 Willson Street. The described scope includes potential roof replacement or modification, replacement of HVAC equipment and distribution systems, and installation of a geoexchange heat pump system and photovoltaic solar panels to decarbonize and electrify the building. The City has applied for an MSBA grant; any project costs exceeding the grant remain the City's responsibility.

stories.name: Szetela Lane Phase 3 DEP Environmental Cleanup

events.summary: The FY27 Capital Outlay Fund includes $200,000 for Phase 3 of environmental remediation at Szetela Lane, managed by the Public Services department in accordance with DEP requirements. This is the third identified phase of the cleanup effort.

City Council referred a $5,723,341 short-term capital appropriation covering more than 60 discrete FY2027 projects across departments. Notable items include $250,000 for a Master Plan update, $207,822 for terminal building design phase, $200,000 each for Pioneer Village renovation and Salem Common Bandstand, $200,000 for Salem Police Headquarters general repairs and upgrades, $156,333 for anti-vehicle bollards at Salem Common, and multiple road design, school, library, parks, and transportation projects.

stories.name: FY2027 Water/Sewer Fund Retained Earnings Capital Projects

events.summary: City Council referred a $1,080,000 appropriation from water/sewer retained earnings for specific utility repair projects: $650,000 for Chestnut Street utility repairs from Summer to Cambridge Streets, $200,000 for water infrastructure repair and replacement, $130,000 for Winthrop Street sewer repairs, and $100,000 for sewer infrastructure repair.

stories.name: Residential Addition, Deck, and Stormwater Work — 18 May Street

events.summary: Monica, Dale, and Oe Onusseit are seeking Conservation Commission approval (file 40-1609) for a residential addition, deck with stairs, landscaping, and stormwater mitigation and management at 18 May Street. The project is within or adjacent to a wetland resource area, requiring ConCom review.

stories.name: Soil Testing and Remediation at Lighthouse Point — Chandler Hovey Park
stories.address: Chandler Hovey Park / Lighthouse Point, Marblehead, MA

contacts.name: Town of Marblehead – Department of Public Works

events.summary: The Town of Marblehead is before the Conservation Commission (file 40-1605) for a public hearing on soil testing and environmental remediation at Lighthouse Point within Chandler Hovey Park. The United States Coast Guard is listed as a named party alongside the Town, suggesting federal involvement in or responsibility for the environmental work at this coastal public-park site.

Accessory dwelling unit application at 6 Broadmere Way, continued from both March 10, 2026 and April 14, 2026 with evidence previously taken. Applicant is Richard Spenceley/OTR Realty; the ADU constitutes a new residential unit.
Sign Design Inc. requests a variance under Section 37.3 to install aggregate signage of 249 sq ft at 100 Independence Way (C-3 zone), exceeding the by-right allowance of 234 sq ft.

stories.name: MS4 Stormwater Permit Compliance Zoning Bylaw Amendments

brief.what: The Town of Swampscott, MA is pursuing zoning bylaw amendments to support compliance with its MS4 stormwater permit, covering parking reductions via special permit, green infrastructure in landscaped parking islands, updated compact-car stall and permeable-paving standards, and overlay-district dimensional changes.
brief.status: The proposal is at the informational stage; the Planning Board heard a presentation from Town DPW and consultant Kleinfelder in May 2026 and expressed general support for the policy direction but requested further review of the redlined bylaw language before taking formal action.
brief.whats_next: The Planning Board is expected to complete its review of the redlined language, with the amendments targeted for consideration at the December 2026 Town Meeting.
brief.outlook: Two consecutive informational hearings with no recorded opposition and a supportive Planning Board signal steady forward momentum; the principal risk is procedural — if the redline review surfaces disagreements or requires additional public process, the December 2026 Town Meeting window could tighten.

contacts.name: Town of Swampscott Department of Public Works

events.summary: Kleinfelder consultants, introduced by DPW Assistant Engineer Mark Noonan, presented proposed zoning bylaw amendments to support Swampscott's MS4 stormwater permit compliance: parking reductions via Special Permit where transit or shared parking is nearby, green infrastructure (rain gardens, bioretention) within landscaped parking islands, and updated compact-car stall and permeable-paving standards in the Smart Growth and Glover Multifamily Overlay Districts. No formal action was taken; amendments are expected to proceed through Planning Board review before consideration at the December 2026 Town Meeting.

Town DPW and consultant Kleinfelder presented proposed zoning bylaw amendments to advance MS4 stormwater permit compliance by reducing impervious cover and promoting green infrastructure. Key proposals include allowing parking reductions via special permit, permitting landscaped parking islands to function as rain gardens or bioswales, updating overlay-district parking dimensional standards, and allowing permeable paving. Board expressed general support for the policy direction but requested further review of the redlined language before any formal action.

Planning Board unanimously approved a Site Plan Special Permit for an 828 sq ft accessory dwelling unit addition and deck at 3 Robin Lane within the Floodplain Overlay District. The ADU is designed for accessible living accommodations to meet a medical need, incorporating kitchen and bathroom facilities, sump pump infrastructure, and drainage improvements to limit new impervious surface. Approval is subject to standard conditions and compliance with floodplain and building requirements.

Proposed amendments to the Swampscott Zoning Bylaw, prepared by Kleinfelder and the Department of Public Works, to achieve MS4 stormwater permit compliance by reducing impervious surface area and promoting green infrastructure. Item is before the Planning Board for discussion.

stories.name: South Essex Sewerage District Capital Infrastructure Program

events.summary: SESD presented to the Select Board on approximately $390 million in priority capital infrastructure needs, including replacing aging wastewater treatment systems beyond their useful life and critical pipeline infrastructure such as the harbor crossing serving Beverly and Danvers. The district is seeking special state legislation for Proposition 2½ relief and borrowing authority; the Board requested strict financial guardrails, community-specific cost projections, and a robust public education effort before lending formal support.

stories.name: National Grid Utility Project — Conant, Berry, Alden, and Park Streets
stories.address: Conant Street, Berry Street, Alden Street, Park Street, Danvers, MA

events.summary: National Grid has begun a 12-week utility project on Conant, Berry, Alden, and Park Streets in Danvers, with both overnight and daytime work phases and on-site police details.

stories.name: Essex Tech Purchase of Waco Building

events.summary: Select Board Member Djoko reported attending Essex Tech's information session on its purchase of the Waco building, where discussions covered the school's office-space needs, parking coordination on Preston Street, and winter sidewalk maintenance for students.

The Preservation Commission completed a revision to Danvers's demolition bylaw (Article 14), aimed at streamlining the demolition-review process for property owners. The update is headed to Town Meeting for a vote.
The Affordable Housing Committee continues work on the Danvers Housing Production Plan, which is expected to come before the Select Board in June 2026.

stories.name: Request for Written Responses for Wayfinding Signs

events.summary: Danvers issued Bid No. 2026-26 seeking written responses for wayfinding signs as part of a downtown improvements project. Submission deadline was May 7, 2026 at 11:00 AM; bid documents available via ProjectDog project code 872929.

stories.name: Forest River Residences Phase 2 (WinnDevelopment)
stories.address: 8 Harrison Road, 9 Harrison Road, 262 Loring Ave, and 266 Loring Ave, Salem, MA

brief.what: WinnDevelopment Companies, LLC proposes up to 145 senior housing units on the former Salem State University South Campus in Salem, MA, through adaptive reuse of three historic campus buildings (Harrington, Academic, and Alumni) and construction of one new four-story building along Harrison Road, with approximately 159 surface parking spaces and historic tax credits. The project occupies 8 Harrison Road, 9 Harrison Road, 262 Loring Ave, and 266 Loring Ave.
brief.status: The public hearing is continued, most recently carried to May 21, 2026, pending engineering peer review and Design Review Board approval of plans.
brief.whats_next: The Planning Board is scheduled to take up the continued hearing on May 21, 2026, where engineering peer review results and Design Review Board plan approval are expected to be addressed.
brief.outlook: The joint Planning Board and Design Review Board hearing closed 8-0 in April, signaling broad early support; with two continuations so far focused on technical review rather than substantive opposition, the project appears on a steady, if methodical, path toward a final approval vote.

contacts.name: Joseph C. Correnti, Esq. f/b/o WinnDevelopment Companies, LLC

events.summary: Joint public hearing closed 8-0 by both the Planning Board and Design Review Board for Phase 2 of the Forest River Residences on the former Salem State University South Campus. WinnDevelopment proposes up to 145 senior housing units through adaptive reuse of three historic campus buildings (Harrington, Academic, and Alumni) and construction of one new four-story building along Harrison Road, with 159 surface parking spaces. The project will receive historic tax credits and is coordinated with the adjacent AvalonBay Phase 1; no final approval vote was taken at this session.

Continued public hearing for Phase 1 of the Forest River Residences, with AvalonBay proposing a 340-unit residential building, a 524-space above-grade parking garage, and a half-acre central landscaped common on the former Salem State University campus. Updated plans address 2070 flood elevation analysis, accessible pathway grading, raised crosswalk treatments, and building signage; continued to April 23, 2026.

Continued public hearing for WinnDevelopment's up to 145-unit Forest River Residences Phase 2, proposing adaptive reuse of three former Salem State University South Campus buildings (Harrington, Academic, and Alumni) plus a new four-story residential building along Harrison Road. The project includes approximately 159 off-street surface parking spaces and landscaped pedestrian connections; continued to May 21, 2026, pending engineering peer review and Design Review Board approval of plans.

brief.what: CAS Salem, LLC seeks to amend a 2021 Site Plan Review and Special Permits for a commercial building at 4 Franklin Street in Salem's North River Canal Corridor, reducing office space from two stories to one story and extending an ambulance garage, with revised parking, lighting, landscaping, and utilities.
brief.status: The Planning Board approved the amendment on May 7, 2026; a second phase on the North River side of the site was explicitly deferred for future redevelopment.
brief.whats_next: No next step is stated in the record beyond the deferred North River phase, which the record describes as remaining for future redevelopment.
brief.outlook: The approval came after two continuations over roughly four weeks, with no noted opposition in the record, suggesting the project is well-positioned to advance to permitting. The deferred North River phase is a likely future Planning Board matter, though the record gives no indication of timing.

events.summary: Public hearing for an amendment to a 2021 Site Plan Review, Flood Hazard Special Permit, and North River Canal Corridor Special Permit decision at 4 Franklin Street, reducing office space from two stories to one story and extending an ambulance garage. Updated building facade with simplified colors and trellis elements, HVAC relocated to rooftop, and revised landscaping and utilities were presented; engineering peer review is still pending and the item was continued to April 23, 2026.

CAS Salem, LLC seeks to amend a 2021 site plan approval at 4 Franklin Street in the North River Canal Corridor, reducing office space from two stories to one story and extending the ambulance garage, with revised parking layout, lighting, landscaping, and utilities; a second phase on the North River side is deferred. The hearing was continued to May 7, 2026.

Planning Board approved an amendment to the 2021 Site Plan Review and Special Permits for 4 Franklin Street, modifying the building to reduce office space from two stories to one story and extend the ambulance garage. Site revisions include updated parking layout, lighting, landscaping, and utilities; a second phase on the North River side of the site remains for future redevelopment.

brief.what: Noble Horizon Homes LLC (Jordan Ryan) sought site plan approval to legalize eight residential units within an existing building at 170 North Street, Salem, MA — a property that has operated with eight units since 1999 despite being permitted for five, including a noncompliant basement unit. Proposed compliance work included adding an egress well to the basement unit, installing a new fire protection line, and providing 16 parking spaces at the rear.
brief.status: The Planning Board approved the site plan review on May 7, 2026, clearing the path for the applicant to bring the building into full compliance following prior permitting difficulties under the previous owner.
brief.whats_next: No next step stated in the record.
brief.outlook: With Planning Board approval in hand, the project appears well-positioned to move into permitting and construction; the trajectory of the hearings — two continuances followed by approval — suggests no unresolved board opposition, though compliance work still needs to clear the building-permit and inspection process.

contacts.name: Jordan Ryan D/B/A Noble Horizon Homes LLC

events.summary: Public hearing for site plan review to legalize eight existing residential units at 170 North Street following a ZBA variance; the building has operated with eight units since 1999 despite being previously permitted for five, including a noncompliant basement unit. Proposed work includes adding an egress well to bring the basement unit into compliance and installing a new fire protection line; continued to April 23, 2026 pending engineering peer review and Design Review Board referral.

Noble Horizon Homes LLC proposes to create 8 residential units within an existing building at 170 North Street in the R2 and Entrance Corridor Overlay districts, with 16 parking spaces including an existing detached garage. The applicant did not appear and the site plan review hearing was continued to May 21, 2026.

Planning Board approved Site Plan Review for 8 residential units within an existing building at 170 North Street, along with 16 parking spaces at the rear including an existing detached garage. Property is in the R2 and Entrance Corridor Overlay zoning districts; applicant is bringing the building into compliance following prior permitting difficulties under the previous owner.

stories.name: 25 Bow Street / 32 Ord Street Lot Boundary Adjustment
stories.address: 25 Bow Street and 32 Ord Street, Salem, MA

brief.what: 25 Bow Salem, LLC sought ZBA approval to split an existing R1 lot into two nonconforming parcels—a 5,008-sq-ft lot at 25 Bow Street retaining an existing home and a 6,909-sq-ft lot at 32 Ord Street for a new single-family dwelling—along with a special permit for a second-story deck at 25 Bow Street built within the north setback prior to approval.
brief.status: The ZBA approved the lot boundary adjustment, dimensional variances for frontage, width, and lot area on both parcels, and the special permit for the deck at its May 6, 2026 special meeting, with conditions requiring deed-recorded privacy fencing and arborvitae plantings negotiated with abutters.
brief.whats_next: No next step stated in the record.
brief.outlook: With full ZBA approval in hand and abutter conditions negotiated, the project appears positioned to move into permitting and construction; the pre-built deck suggests the owner is motivated to move quickly, though the cease-and-desist history may prompt closer scrutiny from the building department.

events.summary: Petition continued to April 15, 2026 to adjust lot boundaries between 25 Bow Street and 32 Ord Street, yielding two nonconforming residential lots (5,008 sq ft and 6,909 sq ft) with deficient frontage, width, and lot area per dwelling unit. The petition also includes a special permit for a new second-story deck within the north setback of 25 Bow Street; an immediate abutter objected to the deck's privacy impact.

25 Bow Salem, LLC is seeking dimensional variance relief to reconfigure two nonconforming R1 lots—a 5,008 sq ft parcel on Bow Street and a 6,909 sq ft parcel on Ord Street (required minimum 15,000 sq ft)—along with a special permit for a second-story deck at 25 Bow Street within its north setback. A cease-and-desist was issued after the deck was constructed prior to ZBA approval; the board continued the petition to its May 6, 2026 special meeting for further review of Planning Board sequencing and abutter privacy concerns.

The ZBA approved a variance and special permit allowing 25 Bow Salem, LLC to subdivide an existing R1 lot into two parcels — a 5,008-sq-ft lot along Bow Street (retaining the existing home) and a 6,909-sq-ft lot along Ord Street (for a new single-family dwelling) — both receiving dimensional relief for frontage, width, and lot area per dwelling unit. A second-story deck on the Bow Street parcel was also approved, subject to deed-recorded privacy fencing and arborvitae conditions negotiated with abutters.

brief.what: CarFive Motors LLC applied for a special permit to add motor vehicle general repair and bodywork use to an existing automobile dealership at 296 Highland Avenue in Salem, MA, located in the B2 / Entrance Corridor Overlay District.
brief.status: The Zoning Board of Appeals approved the special permit on May 6, 2026, with conditions requiring reduction of the southern curb cut per ordinance and downward-facing light fixtures adjacent to residential properties; no change to the building footprint or parking lot was approved.
brief.whats_next: No next step stated in the record.
brief.outlook: The project cleared its only recorded hurdle without public opposition and received approval on its first substantive hearing date, suggesting straightforward implementation of the permitted use.

events.summary: CarFive Motors LLC is seeking a special permit to add motor vehicle general repair and body work use to an existing automobile dealership at 296 Highland Avenue in the B2 / Entrance Corridor Overlay District. The matter was continued to the May 6, 2026 special meeting with no public comment received.

The ZBA approved a special permit allowing CarFive Motors LLC to add motor vehicle general repair and bodywork use to an existing automobile dealership at 296 Highland Avenue (B2 zone, Entrance Corridor Overlay District), with no change to the building footprint or parking lot. Special conditions require reduction of the southern curb cut per ordinance and downward-facing lights on fixtures adjacent to residential properties.

stories.name: 97 Boston Street Pet Grooming and Daycare Use Change

events.summary: The ZBA denied a special permit that would have converted a nonconforming medical training and certification use at 97 Boston Street (R2 zone, Entrance Corridor Overlay District) to a pet grooming and daycare business with a fenced outdoor pet playground at the rear. The board cited absent site plans, parking deficiencies, and noise incompatibility with the surrounding dense residential neighborhood; five abutters testified in opposition and the motion failed 0–5.

stories.name: Kennedy Accessory Dwelling Unit Addition — 7 Hathaway Avenue

events.summary: Beverly Conservation Commission opened and continued a Notice of Intent hearing for a 1,000 sq ft accessory dwelling unit addition with breezeway connection at 7 Hathaway Avenue, situated near a perennial stream and bordering vegetated wetland. The project also includes a two-car driveway (~650 sq ft), a 10x19 deck, and two drywells (1,000 and 500 gallons) to manage approximately 1,700 sq ft of new impervious surface; three replacement trees and an upland meadow habitat are proposed as mitigation. Hearing continued to May 19, 2026.

Ruthy LLC seeks a Special Permit for a new addition to a pre-existing non-conforming mixed-use building in the B1 District, continued from February 24, 2026 with no evidence yet taken. The proposal would create deficiencies in lot area, open area, and parking, exceed height limits, and exceed the 10% expansion cap for non-conforming buildings.

237 Washington Street LLC seeks a Special Permit to convert two existing commercial units into a converted dwelling with one residential unit on the second floor in the Business Residential District. The proposal would result in deficiencies in lot area, frontage, width, side yard setback, open area, and parking.

Riptide Holdings LLC seeks a Special Permit to convert an existing restaurant to a mixed commercial/residential use, adding two dwelling units on the second floor, in the B1 and Business Residential Districts. The proposal would result in deficiencies in lot area, frontage, width, side yard setback, open area, and parking.

The Beverly Planning Board unanimously approved Site Plan Review #170-26 and Special Permit #205-26 for 46 Federal Street LLC to demolish the existing dwelling and garage and construct five ownership-tenure townhouse units on an 8,000 sf lot with a 2,500 sf building footprint, approximately 37 feet tall. The special permit allows a 20-foot driveway aisle width in lieu of the required 24 feet; the ZBA had previously granted a dimensional variance on January 28, 2026. Approval is conditioned on requirements from the Parking & Traffic Commission, Board of Health, Design Review Board, and Engineering Department, and plans are drawn by Seger Architects.

The Commission reviewed the status of an enforcement order at 35 Doty Avenue requiring the applicant to remove a 1,200 sq ft sports court and restore lawn, relocate granite wetland boundary markers, plant 375 sq ft of upland shrubs, and install a 100 sq ft pollinator garden. Corrective work is expected by June 6, after which a site visit will be scheduled and a Certificate of Compliance issued to close out the enforcement order.

stories.name: Definitive Subdivision Plan of 15 Crosby Street

brief.what: 15 Crosby LLC applied to subdivide one existing lot at 15 Crosby Street in Salem, MA into two lots, with a waiver of the Subdivision Control Law frontage requirement for Lot B.
brief.status: The Planning Board approved the definitive subdivision plan and frontage waiver 8-0 with one abstention on April 23, 2026, conditioned on updated contour and grade information being incorporated into the civil plans prior to submission.
brief.whats_next: 15 Crosby LLC must add the required contour and grade information to the civil plans and submit the updated drawings to satisfy the approval condition.
brief.outlook: With an 8-0 vote and a narrow, well-defined condition remaining, the project appears on a straightforward path to final plan submission. The condition is technical rather than substantive, suggesting little remaining friction.

contacts.name: Scott Grover, Esq. f/b/o 15 Crosby LLC

events.summary: Continuance of a public hearing for a Definitive Subdivision Plan to split one existing lot at 15 Crosby Street into two lots, with a requested waiver of the Subdivision Control Law frontage requirement for Lot B.

Continued public hearing for a definitive subdivision to split one existing lot at 15 Crosby Street into two lots, with a requested waiver of frontage requirements for Lot B. Updated stormwater, grading, and sewer connection plans were presented; board cannot approve without complete contour plans, and the item was continued to April 23, 2026.

15 Crosby LLC received approval of a definitive subdivision plan to split one existing lot into two lots at 15 Crosby Street, including a waiver of frontage requirements for Lot B. Both the waivers and the decision passed 8-0 with one abstention, conditioned on updated contour and grade information being added to the civil plans prior to submission.

brief.what: ZXE, LLC proposes to demolish existing buildings at 45 Jefferson Avenue in Salem, MA and construct a four-story, approximately 100,000-square-foot self-storage facility with 735 storage units (721 interior, 14 exterior-accessible) and 11 parking spaces in the Industrial, Coastal Resiliency Overlay, and Entrance Corridor Overlay districts.
brief.status: The Planning Board granted site plan approval by a 9-0 vote on April 23, 2026, with conditions from the City Engineering Department tied to demolition, building permit, and certificate of occupancy milestones; a Design Review Board positive recommendation, Waiver of Demolition Delay, and Order of Conditions were already in hand at the time of approval.
brief.whats_next: The applicant must satisfy City Engineering Department conditions at the demolition, building permit, and certificate of occupancy stages before proceeding through construction.
brief.outlook: The unanimous board vote and the pre-assembled approvals — Design Review Board sign-off, Waiver of Demolition Delay, and Order of Conditions secured before the final hearing — suggest strong procedural momentum with no apparent remaining land-use hurdles; the project appears likely to move into permitting and construction in the near term.

events.summary: ZXE LLC proposes to demolish existing buildings at 45 Jefferson Avenue and construct a new four-story, approximately 100,000-square-foot self-storage facility with 735 storage units (721 interior, 14 exterior-accessible) and 11 parking spaces. The project has received Design Review Board approval; one peer reviewer is expected to submit a final response to the city the following week, with a vote targeted for the April 9, 2026 meeting.

Continued public hearing for demolition of existing buildings at 45 Jefferson Avenue and construction of a new four-story, approximately 100,000-square-foot self-storage facility with 735 storage units (721 interior, 14 exterior) and 11 parking spaces in the Industrial, Coastal Resiliency Overlay, and Entrance Corridor Overlay Districts. Item was continued to April 23, 2026 at the applicant's request without substantive board discussion.

ZXE, LLC received site plan approval (9-0) to demolish existing buildings and construct a new four-story, approximately 100,000 sq ft self-storage facility with 735 units (721 interior, 14 exterior) at 45 Jefferson Avenue in the Industrial, Coastal Resiliency Overlay, and Entrance Corridor Overlay districts. The approved decision incorporates conditions from the City Engineering Department tied to demolition, building permit, and certificate of occupancy milestones, along with a Design Review Board positive recommendation, Waiver of Demolition Delay, and Order of Conditions already in hand.

City Council authorized the Mayor to execute 20-year contracts with BlueWave, a Boston-based solar developer, to deliver energy savings from solar projects to income-eligible residential customers in Salem through the city's municipal aggregation program. A revolving fund may also be established to collect BlueWave incentive payments and reimburse the current aggregation supplier, First Point Power, for discounts it extends to eligible residents.

stories.name: 3A & 5 Winter Island Road ANR Endorsement
stories.address: 3A Winter Island Road / 5 Winter Island Road, Salem, MA

events.summary: Peter Haywood received 9-0 endorsement of an Approval Not Required (ANR) plan to incorporate land from an abutting parcel at 5 Winter Island Road into his existing yacht yard at 3A Winter Island Road, following the abutter's decision to sell. No new lots or structures are proposed.

brief.what: Albert Turner is seeking a variance at 26 Mason Street (RMD district) in Beverly, MA to construct a new detached single-car garage with an accessory dwelling unit on the second floor, under Beverly Zoning Ordinance §§300-36 D(6) and D(8).
brief.status: The variance hearing opened March 25, 2026 and was continued by the Zoning Board of Appeals at the April 22, 2026 session without a vote.
brief.whats_next: No next step stated in the record.
brief.outlook: A continuation after a single hearing is routine for variance applications and does not signal opposition, but the board has not yet acted; whether the project advances will depend on what outstanding questions prompted the continuation.

events.summary: New public hearing on a variance application to construct a new detached single-car garage with an accessory dwelling unit (ADU) on the second floor, pursuant to Sections 300-36 D(6) and D(8) of the Beverly Zoning Ordinance. Property is in the RMD zoning district; no further scope detail is provided in the agenda.

Albert Turner seeks a variance at 26 Mason Street (RMD district) to construct a new detached single-car garage with an accessory dwelling unit (ADU), per Sections 300-36 D(6) and D(8) of the Beverly Zoning Ordinance.

Continued variance hearing for construction of a new detached single-car garage with an Accessory Dwelling Unit above at 26 Mason Street in the RMD district, per Beverly Zoning Ordinance §§300-36 D(6) and D(8).

brief.what: Jason Irving and 21 Bridge Street LLC are seeking a zoning variance to subdivide the existing lot at 21 Bridge Street in Beverly, MA's R10 district into two lots — retaining the current single-family home and creating a new lot for construction of an additional single-family home.
brief.status: The Zoning Board of Appeals hearing has been continued across at least three sessions, most recently on April 22, 2026, with no decision reached and no new continuation date stated in the record.
brief.whats_next: No next step or continuation date is stated in the record.
brief.outlook: Three consecutive continuations without a stated next date suggest the application is unresolved and may be facing procedural or substantive friction; forward momentum appears slow until the board schedules and closes the hearing.

events.summary: Continued hearing on a variance application to subdivide the existing lot, retaining the current single-family home and creating a new second lot for construction of an additional single-family home in the R10 district. The applicant represents the new lot and home are consistent with the general character and lot sizes of the surrounding neighborhood.

Jason Irving and 21 Bridge Street LLC seek a variance to subdivide 21 Bridge Street (R10 district) into two lots, retaining the existing single-family home and creating a new lot for the construction of an additional single-family home. The applicant characterizes the proposal as consistent with the neighborhood's general character and surrounding lot sizes. This is a continued public hearing with no new continuation date stated.

Continued variance hearing to subdivide the existing lot at 21 Bridge Street, retain the existing single-family home, and create a new second lot for construction of an additional single-family home consistent with neighborhood character in the R10 district.

stories.name: Boston Consignment Retail and Storage Use Variance

contacts.name: Joseph P. Coughlin, Trustee of Gateway II Trust of 1997

events.summary: Variance requested to allow approximately 9,000 sq.ft. of an existing 51,158 sq.ft. building for retail use by Boston Consignment (70% of business conducted online) and 12,000 sq.ft. for associated storage and office use in the IR zoning district.

contacts.name: Cellco Partnership d/b/a Verizon Wireless and the Towers, LLC

events.summary: Continued hearing for a Special Permit under §300-42(C)(1)(p) and height and setback variances under §300-25A to install a wireless communications facility with a 124-foot monopole (including 4-foot lightning rod) at approximately 52-foot setback from the property line in the IR district, Map 68, Parcel 25B.

Public hearing on a City of Salem Notice of Intent for in-kind seawall repairs and landside erosion restoration at the Lead Mills seawall on Lafayette St, a publicly owned coastal infrastructure site within a wetlands-protected area. No cost figures are stated.

The Town of Danvers solicited bids for perennial and annual bed maintenance services (Bid No. 2026-02). The bid closed April 19, 2026; documents and submission are handled electronically through ProjectDog at project code 873461.

stories.name: 25 Washington Square North Bed and Breakfast

brief.what: Jeremy Schwartz and Shelby Soloff are converting 25 Washington Square North in Salem, MA into a six-guest-room, owner-occupied bed and breakfast, with no exterior structural changes proposed.
brief.status: The Zoning Board of Appeals approved a parking variance and special permit on April 15, 2026, subject to a condition requiring documentation of at least three leased off-street spaces within a half mile during September through November 15.
brief.whats_next: No next step stated in the record.
brief.outlook: With ZBA approval in hand, the project appears well-positioned to move into interior buildout; the seasonal parking condition is the primary ongoing compliance obligation and could require attention before a fall opening.

events.summary: Petition continued to April 15, 2026 to operate a bed and breakfast at 25 Washington Square North. The applicants seek a parking variance (2 on-site spaces vs. 8 required, with 6 off-site spaces within a quarter mile) and a special permit for the B&B use; no exterior structural changes are proposed.

The ZBA approved a parking variance and special permit for Jeremy Schwartz and Shelby Soloff to operate a proposed six-guest-room, owner-occupied bed and breakfast at 25 Washington Square North. Two on-site parking spaces were approved where eight are required, subject to a special condition requiring documentation of at least three leased off-street spaces within a half mile during September–November 15. Interior work includes bathroom additions and removals, conversion of fireplaces to gas, and installation of sprinklers and fire suppression systems.

stories.name: 33-37 Osborne Street Auto Storage and Office Redevelopment

brief.what: Justin Cook received ZBA approval to demolish an existing nonconforming heavy industrial structure on a half-acre site at 33-37 Osborne Street in Salem, MA and construct a new two-story building for personal automobile storage (51-vehicle capacity with car elevator) on the first floor and business offices on the second floor, with 16 exterior parking spaces.
brief.status: The ZBA approved the special permit on April 15, 2026; the historic commission previously waived a demolition delay for the existing structure.
brief.whats_next: Planning Board approval is required before the project can proceed.
brief.outlook: The project cleared its main zoning hurdle without apparent opposition and has a demolition-delay waiver in hand, suggesting it is on a relatively clear path — though Planning Board review remains an open variable.

events.summary: Special permit petition continued to April 15, 2026 to replace a nonconforming heavy industrial use at 33-37 Osborne Street with a new two-story building featuring first-floor automobile storage and second-floor business offices. The proposal involves clearing the entire existing site and constructing a new structure that complies with dimensional and parking requirements.

The ZBA approved a special permit for Justin Cook to demolish an existing nonconforming heavy industrial structure on a half-acre site at 33-37 Osborne Street and construct a new two-story building for personal automobile storage (capacity 51 vehicles, with car elevator) and second-floor business offices, with 16 exterior parking spaces and modest landscaping. No vehicle repair will occur on-site; the historic commission previously waived a demolition delay for the existing structure, and further Planning Board approval is required.

Continued petition at 102 Margin Street (R2 district) for Variances and a Special Permit to construct a rear addition and staircase encroaching into side and rear setbacks. Relief is also sought for lot area per dwelling unit (1,283 sq ft) and a parking reduction from 3 to 2 spaces, indicating a multi-unit residential property.

stories.name: 25 Bow Street and 32 Ord Street Lot Reconfiguration
stories.address: 25 Bow Street and 32 Ord Street, Salem, MA 01970

events.summary: Continued petition by 25 Bow Salem, LLC for Variance and Special Permit relief on two abutting R1 parcels with modified lot boundaries: a 5,008 sq ft Bow Street lot and a 6,909 sq ft Ord Street lot, both deficient in frontage, width, and lot area per dwelling unit. The Special Permit would authorize a new second-story deck within Bow Street's north setback.

stories.name: CarFive Motors 296 Highland Avenue Special Permit

events.summary: First hearing on a Special Permit for CarFive Motors LLC to add motor vehicle general repair and body work to an existing automobile dealership at 296 Highland Avenue in the B2/Entrance Corridor Overlay District. No structural changes to the building are described; the request addresses the use expansion only.

The Committee on Ordinances, Licenses and Legal Affairs discussed a draft zoning ordinance amendment that would expand allowable home occupations in Salem, permitting residents to operate small businesses (visual arts, recording studios, hair styling, family day care, etc.) from their homes without a special permit, subject to standards for appearance, activity, noise, parking, and storage. The committee debated enforceability questions — including square-footage caps (20% vs. 25% of gross floor area), shipment limits, accessory structures, and prohibited uses — but did not reach consensus on draft language. The item was kept in committee 5-0 pending a future meeting with city department heads including the building inspector and city solicitor.

The ZBA held an informal check-in on a 2024 special permit (Docket 24-5085) for conversion of a circa-1900 single-family home into a two-unit duplex; members found the as-built structure deviated from approved plans with two front doors, a substantially altered roofline, and a side-by-side rather than front-to-back unit layout. By board consensus—no formal vote taken—the project was allowed to proceed as built, citing cross-department miscommunication and the unreasonable hardship of requiring structural redesign at the interior-finish stage.

The ZBA unanimously approved a Finding under Section 3.10 allowing 20 Water LLC to substitute a former restaurant (pre-existing non-conforming use) with a by-appointment recording studio at this R-1 property. No footprint changes are involved; approval was conditioned on hours of 9 AM–8 PM and sign lighting off one hour after closing.

The Planning Board unanimously approved a Site Plan Special Permit for demolition of the existing structure at 69 Puritan Road and construction of a new approximately 3,000-square-foot single-family residence with attached garage and in-ground pool in the A-3 Zoning District. The project carries prior approvals from the Conservation and Historical Commissions and is designed to comply with floodplain requirements by elevating the structure above base flood elevation. The Board noted concerns about the extent of paved surfaces and dual curb cuts in the front yard and recommended reducing impervious coverage and enhancing landscaping.

stories.name: Zoning Bylaw Amendment – Site Plan Special Permit (Section 5.4.0.0)

events.summary: The Planning Board will discuss proposed amendments to Zoning Bylaw Section 5.4.0.0 (Site Plan Special Permit), including revisions to Sections 5.4.3.0 (Procedures) and 5.4.4.0 (Preparation of Plans) to accommodate online permit applications, and the introduction of new Sections 5.4.10.0–5.4.10.6 establishing an Administrative Site Plan Modification process.

brief.what: Mobile home park expansion at 96–98 Newbury Street, Danvers, MA, proposed by Steven Tedesco of Temar Properties, LLC. The project involves new mobile homes, stormwater improvements (detention basins, forebays, manholes), and pavement within the 100-foot wetland buffer zone, with ZBA and Planning Board approvals already secured before the Conservation Commission proceedings.
brief.status: The Conservation Commission issued an Order of Conditions on April 9, 2026 approving two new mobile homes and expanded pavement (DEP #14-1449), with a waiver granted for 280 square feet of pavement within the 50-foot no-build zone, conditioned on a $2,500 debris-cleanup bond and installation of a stone infiltration trench; one commissioner dissented.
brief.whats_next: No next step stated in the record.
brief.outlook: All three local permitting bodies have now approved the project, suggesting the applicant is positioned to proceed to construction; the single dissenting vote and bonded conditions are minor friction, not apparent blockers, though the reduction in approved scope from the originally proposed ~24 units to two reflects some negotiation with regulators.

events.summary: The Conservation Commission continued the Notice of Intent hearing for a mobile home park expansion at 96–98 Newbury Street to April 9, 2026, pending a site visit. The project proposes approximately 24 new units, stormwater improvements (detention basins, forebays, manholes), four utility poles, and minor pavement addition within the 100-foot wetland buffer. A no-build zone waiver covering roughly 2,650 sq ft (~11%) of the 35–50 ft zone for stormwater facilities was also presented; ZBA and Planning Board approvals are already in hand.

Conservation Commission issued an Order of Conditions approving two new mobile homes and expanded pavement at 96-98 Newbury Street (DEP #14-1449). A waiver was granted for 280 square feet of pavement within the 50-foot no-build zone, conditioned on a $2,500 bond for debris cleanup and installation of a stone infiltration trench. One commissioner (Splaine) dissented.

Select Board voted 5-0 to recommend Town Meeting approval of $317,000 for pavement management and sidewalk improvements.
